quotes.verbatim_quote:
Both line studies were located on the lower-most
region of the fingerboard (as have most of the melodic exercises up until now). Starting with line studies 2A
and 2B, the line studies will ascend up the fingerboard with each successive set of line studies.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines the structural organizing principle of the entire Phase III system — each successive pair of line studies moves higher up the neck, systematically covering the full fingerboard.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
NOTE: The same two chord progressions will be utilized for the remaining line studies, according to let-
ter.

i.e.: Line studies 1A, 2A, 3A, 4A and 5A will share the same chord progression. The same holds
true for the “B” studies.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This rule establishes that harmonic context is fixed while only the positional starting point changes, so the student can hear how the same changes sound across different regions of the neck.

quotes.topic: Forms of activity as starting points
quotes.verbatim_quote: Line studies 2A and 2B start with the second form of activity in G minor.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This ties each line-study pair to a numbered positional 'form of activity,' confirming the method's position-system framework as the structural backbone of the studies.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
At the end of this line study, you will notice an arrow. This merely suggests that the line study can continue
onward, starting again on a G minor chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This notation rule tells the student the studies are cyclically extensible, clarifying how to use the material for open-ended improvisation practice rather than treating it as a closed etude.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
studies 5A and 5B have been started and left for you to complete. The studies utilize the same
1s as the preceding eight.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is a pedagogical directive requiring the student to generate their own line studies using the same forms and progressions, converting passive study into active compositional application.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
L. Substitute chords of the same name and quality. _ II. Converting to minor through substitution.

Cmaj6 = Am7 (Relative minor)

Cmaj = Cmaj7 = Cmaj9 etc. Cmaj7 = Am9 (Relative minor)
Cm6 = Cm7 = Cm9 etc. Cmaj9 = Em7 (Secondary relative minor)
C7 = C9 = C13 — ete. C7 = Gm

Cm7b5 = Ebm6
quotes.why_load_bearing: This table is the conceptual core of the entire phase: it gives the specific equivalences that allow complex compositional chords to be recast as minor forms, enabling the method's approach to linear improvisation.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
PROCEDURE

1) Study the song in its original form (page 52).
a) Play the melody.
b) Study the chord changes.
2) On pages 53 and 54 you will find the chord diagrams and the simplified breakdown of the harmonic struc-
ture (lower stave).
a) Learn the chord voicings (provided in both musical and graphic forms).
b) Study the substitutions found immediately below the compositional harmony.
3) Study the analysis on pages 55 through 57.
a) Relate each substitution to the compositional harmony, with the aid of the written analysis.
b) Memorize the substitutions.
4) Examine the sample solo on pages 58 through 62.
a) Play through the sample solo until the study can be performed nonstop.
b) Record the changes on tape and play the solo over them.

5) Now that you’ve learned the tune thoroughly, compose your own solo over the changes, utilizing the sub-
stitutions given or any of your own substitute forms.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This five-step procedure is the method's complete workflow for applying substitution to a real composition, sequencing the student from analysis through memorization to original improvisation.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
ERGO: The next logical step of the procedure would be to apply the principles learned in this work to more
common forms of progressions and songs.

EXAMPLES: “Stella by Starlight” ........-.0 002-005 Victor Young
“All the Things You Are”... 2.2.20 eee eee Jerome Kern
“The Days of Wine and Roses”... .- +--+ Henry Mancini
“Summertime” 6... ee eee George Gershwin
“Scrapple From the Apple” ....-..-...-.---- Charlie Parker
quotes.why_load_bearing: This rule directs the student to transfer the substitution method from the abstract study piece to canonical jazz repertoire, completing the bridge between technique exercise and real-world application.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Application through substitution and reduction. As was previously mentioned, complex chord changes can
often be reduced to or substituted by more simplistic minor chord forms. It is by the means of chord substitu-
tion that an original composition titled “Nadine” will be reduced to these minor chord forms for the purpose
of linear improvisation.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This passage states the chapter's central pedagogical concept — that chord substitution is the mechanism linking complex harmony to the minor-form linear improvisation system developed throughout the book.
